waves.title: Software Disruption Now
waves.publicThesis: Software disruption is already underway across writing, coding, support, research, and operations.
waves.narrative: The practical question is not whether a clean AGI day arrives, but how much useful work shifts before the label catches up.
waves.overlap: This wave starts first, but labor and physical-economy effects begin before it is finished.
waves.analystFocus: Track real workflow replacement, utility, and labor effects instead of headline capability alone.
waves.engineerFocus: Reliability, eval quality, tool orchestration, and inference cost determine how much software work actually moves.
gates: Workflow-level evaluation instead of benchmark theater

waves.title: Broad Labor Stress
waves.publicThesis: Labor-market stress becomes broad enough that it is hard to dismiss as isolated sector churn.
waves.narrative: This is a transition period, not automatic collapse: painful reallocation, tighter management, and messy bargaining over where automation actually sticks.
waves.overlap: Software disruption keeps spreading while labor stress rises unevenly across occupations, firms, and regions.
waves.analystFocus: Watch hiring freezes, role compression, wage pressure, and the gap between output growth and headcount.
waves.engineerFocus: Deployment quality matters because partial autonomy changes org charts before it delivers full replacement.
gates: Sustained software deployment in business workflows
Managerial willingness to redesign jobs around AI systems
Institutional responses in training, benefits, and regulation

waves.title: Physical-Economy Disruption
waves.publicThesis: The first plausible window for broad physical-economy disruption is early in the 2030s, through robots, autonomous logistics, and tightly managed deployment.
waves.narrative: Industrial robot momentum is real, but it does not prove that general-purpose humanoids flood the economy tomorrow.
waves.overlap: Physical deployment stacks on top of software and labor shocks rather than waiting for them to conclude.
waves.analystFocus: Separate narrow, high-ROI deployment from general-purpose robotics hype.
waves.engineerFocus: Safety cases, fleet economics, maintenance, energy, and real-world reliability dominate the pace.
gates: High-uptime robotic systems in constrained environments
Autonomous logistics with acceptable incident rates
Energy, parts, and maintenance capacity for fleet rollouts

waves.title: Scenario Territory
waves.publicThesis: Beyond 2035 the right frame is scenarios, not forecasts.
waves.narrative: Energy, supply chains, regulation, and real-world reliability dominate the outer boundary more than abstract capability curves.
waves.overlap: Long-run outcomes remain path-dependent on how the first three waves interact with power, politics, and industrial capacity.
waves.analystFocus: Treat long-run claims as branching scenarios shaped by infrastructure, governance, and social response.
waves.engineerFocus: The bottleneck shifts from model novelty to system integration across energy, hardware, policy, and safety.
gates: Power and chip supply at civilization scale
Governance that can survive uneven deployment

items.headline: Agent benchmarks are improving faster than reliability in adversarial or long-horizon work.
items.summary: Recent agent work expands what software systems can do, but brittle adaptation and execution drift remain visible when environments become dynamic.
items.whyIncluded: It supports the claim that software disruption can spread before anyone can defend a clean AGI threshold story.

public.summary: Software systems are already taking on more writing, coding, and support tasks, but they still fail in operationally important ways.
public.implication: Expect partial automation and labor reshaping before dependable autonomy.
public.caution: Benchmark gains are not the same thing as trustworthy end-to-end job replacement.

analyst.thesisRole: Observed signal for the first wave.
analyst.context: Capability gains are real, yet benchmark-to-work translation remains uneven and should be measured directly in output, staffing, and exception rates.
analyst.watchFor: Sustained gains on messy repository-level work and customer-facing exception handling.

engineer.technicalDetail: Long-horizon planning, environment shifts, and evaluator gaming remain active failure modes even as agent stacks improve.
engineer.architectureNote: Evaluation loops need adversarial conditions, state drift, and human handoff points.
unlocks: Wider deployment in coding and operations

references.title: TraderBench: How Robust Are AI Agents in Adversarial Capital Markets?
SWE-Hub: A Unified Production System for Scalable, Executable Software Engineering Tasks

items.headline: Cheaper frontier-style models widen the set of routine software tasks firms can automate.
items.summary: Model releases that emphasize speed and cost efficiency matter because they lower the threshold for broad workflow deployment.
items.whyIncluded: Software disruption spreads when cost and latency improve enough for ordinary business use, not only when headline benchmarks jump.

public.summary: Faster, cheaper models matter because they make automation practical in everyday workflows, not just demos.
public.implication: The near-term spread of AI will be driven by deployment economics as much as by raw capability.
public.caution: Cheap inference expands usage, but it does not erase supervision, quality control, or integration costs.

analyst.thesisRole: Observed deployment signal for the software wave.
analyst.context: Cost-efficient releases make it easier for firms to experiment with AI across support, research, and operations, increasing real utility before any consensus on AGI.
analyst.watchFor: Falling per-task cost in coding, support, and document workflows.

engineer.technicalDetail: The operational threshold for adoption is often latency, reliability, and price per useful task rather than absolute benchmark standing.
engineer.architectureNote: System design shifts toward orchestration, retrieval, and workflow guardrails when model cost falls.
unlocks: Routine deployment in internal ops and customer support

references.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite: Built for intelligence at scale

items.headline: The right frame for the current moment is utility and labor effect, not waiting for a ceremonial AGI day.
items.summary: The first wave is already visible in software, so the measure that matters is useful work moved and what that does to jobs and organizations.
items.whyIncluded: It is the core interpretive move behind the four-wave refactor.

public.summary: Software shock is the part moving fastest right now.
public.implication: The timeline should foreground work displacement and productivity shifts instead of theatrical AGI countdowns.
public.caution: Real utility can be economically disruptive even when systems are still uneven.

analyst.thesisRole: Core thesis for the first wave.
analyst.context: A single AGI threshold is less informative than evidence of widening deployment, rising task completion rates, and changing labor demand.
analyst.watchFor: Role redesign before formal headcount reduction.

engineer.technicalDetail: Teams can absorb brittle systems if they still deliver enough net output in bounded workflows.
engineer.bottleneck: Measuring useful task completion instead of demo success.
unlocks: A credible read on when software disruption becomes macro-relevant

references.title: TraderBench: How Robust Are AI Agents in Adversarial Capital Markets?

items.headline: The late 2020s are the first plausible window for broad labor stress from cumulative software automation.
items.summary: Once software automation hits enough adjacent roles at once, labor-market stress becomes harder to write off as isolated churn.

citations.label: How Well Does Agent Development Reflect Real-World Work?

items.whyIncluded: This is the second wave in the sober timeline: transition pressure rather than automatic collapse.

public.summary: Broad labor stress can arrive without a net economic collapse.
public.implication: The transition may feel painful because firms can reallocate labor faster than workers can retrain or move.
public.caution: A turbulent reallocation period is not the same as instant permanent unemployment.

analyst.thesisRole: Core thesis for the labor wave.
analyst.context: The relevant threshold is not one model becoming magical. It is enough deployment across related occupations to alter wages, hiring, and bargaining power at once.
analyst.watchFor: Hiring freezes in white-collar support functions paired with stable or rising output.

engineer.technicalDetail: Partial autonomy can compress teams even when full automation is unavailable.

references.title: How Well Does Agent Development Reflect Real-World Work?

items.headline: Research attention is shifting toward whether agent performance maps to real work rather than benchmark abstractions alone.
items.summary: The transition from benchmark discussion to work-mapping is itself a signal that labor exposure is becoming the operational question.

citations.label: How Well Does Agent Development Reflect Real-World Work?

items.whyIncluded: The labor wave should be tied to work exposure and organizational adoption, not only to claims about raw intelligence.

public.summary: The important question is increasingly whether AI systems can do real work that organizations will trust.
public.implication: Labor stress becomes more plausible once the conversation moves from demos to workflow fit.
public.caution: Real-work evidence still has to survive integration, compliance, and management friction.

analyst.thesisRole: Early warning signal for the second wave.
analyst.context: A shift in evaluation focus often precedes a shift in deployment and management decisions.
analyst.watchFor: Published comparisons between benchmark wins and real business task completion.

engineer.technicalDetail: Work-mapping requires evaluators that capture exceptions, interruptions, policy constraints, and collaboration overhead.
engineer.bottleneck: Good task models for real organizations.
unlocks: More credible forecasts of labor exposure

references.title: How Well Does Agent Development Reflect Real-World Work?

items.headline: Management tightening, retraining efforts, and policy fights arrive before any clean long-run equilibrium.
items.summary: Institutions respond to labor stress while the software wave is still unfolding, which makes the transition politically and organizationally messy.
items.whyIncluded: The timeline should present labor stress as a contested transition period rather than a one-step collapse story.

public.summary: Firms and governments will likely improvise through the labor shock rather than meet it with one coherent plan.
public.implication: Expect a stretch of uneven rules, retraining pushes, and disputes over where automation is allowed to land.
public.caution: Policy can slow deployment in some sectors while accelerating it elsewhere.

analyst.thesisRole: Institutional response thesis for the second wave.
analyst.context: Labor-market stress becomes socially salient when organizations redesign roles faster than benefits, training, and bargaining systems adapt.
analyst.watchFor: Sector-specific rules on AI use in education, health, law, and public administration.

engineer.technicalDetail: Compliance, auditability, and fallback design become deployment requirements once AI systems start touching regulated work.
unlocks: Broader but more tightly managed deployment

references.title: The Global Landscape of Environmental AI Regulation

items.headline: Embodied and humanoid benchmarks are improving, but mostly inside constrained tasks and controlled environments.
items.summary: Industrial and embodied progress is real, but the evidence still points to narrow deployment first rather than instant general-purpose coverage.
items.whyIncluded: It anchors the robotics wave in actual momentum while pushing back on humanoid flood assumptions.

public.summary: Robot progress is worth taking seriously, but today it looks more like narrow industrial momentum than universal physical autonomy.
public.implication: The physical-economy shock should be modeled as staged deployment in high-ROI settings first.
public.caution: A better benchmark or demo is not proof of cheap, safe, mass deployment.

analyst.thesisRole: Observed signal for the third wave.
analyst.context: The strongest evidence points toward constrained environments with repeatable tasks, not open-world humanoid substitution at scale.
analyst.watchFor: Warehouse, factory, and logistics deployments with uptime and safety data.

engineer.technicalDetail: Embodied systems face compounding error from sensing, actuation, maintenance, and safety constraints that software-only systems can often bypass.
unlocks: Constrained physical deployment in logistics and industry

references.title: Scaling Tasks, Not Samples: Mastering Humanoid Control through Multi-Task Model-Based Reinforcement Learning
RMBench: Memory-Dependent Robotic Manipulation Benchmark with Insights into Policy Design

items.headline: The first plausible broad physical-economy disruption window opens in the early 2030s, after software disruption is already established.
items.summary: Robots and autonomous logistics can matter economically in the early 2030s without implying instant general-purpose humanoid saturation.
items.whyIncluded: It places the physical-economy shock later than the software shock while still acknowledging real robotics momentum.

public.summary: Physical disruption likely comes later than software disruption.
public.implication: The early 2030s are the first plausible period for broad logistics and industrial effects.
public.caution: Industrial robot progress should not be overstated into a claim that humanoids will flood the whole economy tomorrow.

analyst.thesisRole: Core thesis for the third wave.
analyst.context: The relevant shift is cumulative deployment in constrained physical settings, not a single dramatic robotics reveal.
analyst.watchFor: Real fleet deployment metrics instead of staged demo videos.

engineer.technicalDetail: Physical systems compound software risk with uptime, maintenance, liability, and hardware replacement cycles.
engineer.bottleneck: Economically viable deployment outside lab conditions.

references.title: Scaling Tasks, Not Samples: Mastering Humanoid Control through Multi-Task Model-Based Reinforcement Learning

items.headline: Energy, maintenance, supply chains, and safety cases dominate the pace of physical deployment.
items.summary: The limiting factor for the robotics wave is not just model quality. It is the real-world stack required to keep fleets running safely and cheaply.
items.whyIncluded: It keeps the physical-economy wave anchored in infrastructure instead of science-fiction timelines.

public.summary: The physical-economy wave will be paced by energy, parts, maintenance, and regulation.
public.implication: Even strong robot capability does not translate into instant economy-wide saturation.
public.caution: The bottleneck is industrial capacity and operational reliability, not only smarter models.

analyst.thesisRole: Infrastructure thesis for the third wave.
analyst.context: The gap between a functioning demo and a scaled fleet is where many overconfident physical-economy forecasts fail.
analyst.watchFor: Power availability, unit economics, and service network buildout.

engineer.technicalDetail: Physical AI inherits all the constraints of industrial systems: maintenance windows, safety audits, spare parts, grid power, and local site integration.
engineer.bottleneck: Fleet economics under real service conditions.

references.title: The Global Landscape of Environmental AI Regulation

items.headline: Policy discussion is already expanding toward energy, environmental cost, and governance constraints around advanced AI.
items.summary: Long-run AI outcomes are likely to be shaped by infrastructure and governance debates at least as much as by capability curves.
items.whyIncluded: It is an early signal that the outer boundary after 2035 is constrained by governance and energy, not only by model ambition.

public.summary: Long-run AI outcomes will be constrained by power, regulation, and environmental cost.
public.implication: Past a certain point, governance and infrastructure matter as much as capability progress.
public.caution: Long-run scenarios can diverge widely because these constraints are political and industrial, not only technical.

analyst.thesisRole: Early signal for the scenario territory wave.
analyst.context: The farther out the forecast goes, the more the credible question becomes system integration under policy and infrastructure limits.
analyst.watchFor: Energy use caps, reporting rules, and cross-border compute policy.

engineer.technicalDetail: Outer-boundary deployment depends on whether energy, land, cooling, and regulatory approval can scale with ambition.
unlocks: A wider range of plausible long-run deployment paths

references.title: The Global Landscape of Environmental AI Regulation

items.headline: After 2035 the right framing is branching scenarios, not a single forecast line.
items.summary: Beyond the mid-2030s, small differences in infrastructure, policy, and reliability assumptions produce very different social outcomes.
items.whyIncluded: It is the final wave's core instruction: stop pretending precision is forecast quality.

public.summary: The farther out the timeline goes, the more humility matters.
public.implication: Beyond 2035 the honest move is to compare scenarios, not to promise a single date.
public.caution: False precision is especially misleading once energy, supply chains, and regulation start to dominate the path.

analyst.thesisRole: Core thesis for the fourth wave.
analyst.context: Scenario analysis is more credible than point forecasting once the dominant constraints become political, industrial, and path-dependent.
analyst.watchFor: Divergence between regions with different grid, chip, and regulatory capacity.

engineer.technicalDetail: Long-run technical trajectories are tightly coupled to non-model variables that cannot be extrapolated from benchmark trends alone.
unlocks: A more defensible long-run planning frame

references.title: The Global Landscape of Environmental AI Regulation

items.headline: Long-run divergence depends on the interaction of grid power, chip supply, regulation, and real-world reliability.
items.summary: The outer boundary is set by infrastructure coordination problems that are slower and more political than pure model iteration.
items.whyIncluded: It captures the main caveat behind the final wave: the problem becomes systems integration at civilization scale.

public.summary: Very long-run outcomes hinge on industrial and political capacity, not just capability curves.
public.implication: The same technical frontier can yield very different futures under different energy and governance conditions.
public.caution: Reliability failures or infrastructure scarcity can cap deployment long before abstract capability ceilings are reached.

analyst.thesisRole: Long-run systems thesis for the fourth wave.
analyst.context: Once the timeline moves beyond the mid-2030s, infrastructure variables dominate enough that narrow AI forecasting becomes incomplete.
analyst.watchFor: Power, cooling, and chip availability as macro constraints.

engineer.technicalDetail: The limiting resource becomes the full stack of power, cooling, hardware throughput, maintenance, safety, and regulatory permission.

references.title: The Global Landscape of Environmental AI Regulation
